nativeRange.habitat: Intertidal estuarine and lagoonal shorelines, brackish coastal swamps, and the seaward fringe of tropical and subtropical wetlands. Forms the structural backbone of mangrove forests; propagules detach from parent trees as elongated pencil-shaped seedlings that float on tidal currents to colonize new shorelines.

decorPreferences: refugium / sump chamber or open-top display
minimum 40 cm of vertical air-space clearance above water for leaf growth
bright overhead lighting (PAR 200+ or direct daylight)
stable rooting in fine sand, rubble, or wedged into rockwork
gentle non-laminar flow at the root zone

goodWith: fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) display setups with open tops
avoidWith: closed-top tanks without overhead air clearance
submerging the leaves continuously (kills the plant)
high-flow display zones that uproot the propagule
low-light setups (mangroves require very bright light)

summary: The Red Mangrove (Rhizophora mangle) is a tropical estuarine vascular plant sold as floating pencil-shaped seedlings for use in marine refugia, saltwater paludariums, and open-top displays. It is kept primarily for nutrient export through root uptake and for the structural interest of its developing prop-root system.
careNotes:
The Red Mangrove is the most commonly kept mangrove species in the reef hobby, sold as elongated pencil-shaped floating propagules roughly 15–30 cm long that have already sprouted from the parent tree before detaching. The propagule is technically a viviparous seedling: it germinates while still attached to the adult tree, drops into the water, floats horizontally for weeks while distributing on tides, then orients vertically and roots when it lodges in soft substrate. Propagules are widely stocked by marine and refugium specialty vendors.

CRITICAL SETUP NOTE: Red Mangrove (Rhizophora mangle, family Rhizophoraceae) is a true vascular flowering plant, NOT a macroalga or submerged aquatic. The roots grow in salt or brackish water, but LEAVES MUST STAY ABOVE WATER in a humid atmospheric layer. Submerging the leaves continuously will kill the plant within weeks. For this reason, mangroves are almost always housed in open-top refugia, sump chambers with sufficient vertical air space, or in dedicated paludarium and saltwater pond setups. A sealed-canopy reef display will not work unless a cutout in the canopy gives the shoots room to grow above the water surface.

Planting the propagule: Orient the elongated cigar-shaped propagule vertically with the pointed (root) end down. It can simply be floated until it begins to root on its own — it will sense gravity and reorient vertically after a few days — or wedged into a substrate pocket, rockwork crevice, or a small clay pot of aragonite sand. The bottom 4–8 cm of the propagule should sit in water; the upper 8–20 cm should remain in air and will develop leaves within 2–6 weeks. Once rooted, the propagule begins producing the distinctive prop-root (aerial root) structure the genus is known for.

Water chemistry and salinity: R. mangle is salt-tolerant across a broad range, from pure freshwater up to full ocean salinity (SG 1.025–1.026), and is generally grown in standard reef-tank parameters (SG 1.023–1.026). Salt crystals visible on the leaf surface are normal and indicate healthy salt exchange; spraying the leaves with RODI water once weekly can help dissolve accumulated deposits and keep the leaf pores functioning. The plant tolerates brackish (SG 1.005–1.015) and freshwater conditions as well, though leaf colour and growth rate vary with salinity.

Lighting: Very bright light is required for healthy growth — considerably more than typical macroalgae setups demand. PAR 200+ at the leaf surface is generally advised, ideally from full-spectrum LEDs, a CFL or T5 array dedicated to the refugium, or direct natural daylight from a window or skylight. Insufficient light produces leggy stems with sparse, yellowing leaves and slow root development.

Nutrient export role: Mangroves take up nitrate and phosphate from the water through their root system and transport those nutrients up to the leaves; older leaves accumulate salt and minerals and drop seasonally, carrying those nutrients out of the system with them. The export rate is considerably slower than that of fast-growing macroalgae like Chaetomorpha, so mangroves are generally kept alongside macroalgae for combined nutrient control rather than as the sole export pathway. They tend to be most useful in display refugia where their structural and educational value is appreciated, in mature low-nutrient systems, and in mangrove-biotope displays.

Growth and pruning: Slow-growing — expect 10–30 cm of new shoot growth per year in good conditions. In nature R. mangle reaches 5–20 m tall, but in aquarium use the shoots are typically pruned at 60–120 cm to fit the available air space. Pruning the leader shoot encourages branching. The prop-root structure develops gradually over years.

Pests and concerns: Mangroves grown over saltwater do not typically attract aquarium pests, but the open-top setup required to keep the leaves aerial can lead to airborne pest issues such as fungus gnats or aphids, particularly if the tank is kept near houseplants. Leaf yellowing is usually attributable to insufficient light (PAR too low), salt buildup on the leaf surface (rinse with RODI water), or accidental submergence of the leaves. Sourcing aquacultured propagules is preferred over wild-collected material.
breedingNotes: Propagation in the aquarium context means starting with the elongated viviparous propagules (15–30 cm) that drop from parent trees in late summer and early fall. Propagules from aquacultured stock are available from marine and refugium specialty vendors. Cuttings from mature stems can root but are considerably more difficult and rarely practiced. Flowers and fruit occur only on mature trees several years old, and most aquarium specimens do not reach reproductive maturity.

primary.name: AlgaeBarn — Mangrove Propagule
primary.notes: Refugium-vendor product page documenting the typical sale form (15–30 cm pencil-shaped propagule), positioning above water requirement, nutrient-export role, salinity tolerance, and lighting requirements. AlgaeBarn is one of the largest U.S. refugium-livestock vendors and the source most hobbyists encounter when buying R. mangle for the first time.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply — Mangroves in the Reef Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked the open-top requirement, root-vs-leaf positioning, nutrient export expectations vs. macroalgae alternatives, lighting (PAR 200+), and slow growth-rate. BRS notes mangrove export is slower than Chaetomorpha but useful for display and educational value.

additional.name: Reef Cleaners — Mangroves
additional.notes: Cross-checked the propagule planting procedure (vertical orientation, partial submergence of 4–8 cm), salt-crystal excretion on leaves, propagule floating-and-rooting biology, and the freshwater-to-marine salinity tolerance range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Rhizophora mangle
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Rhizophoraceae, order Malpighiales), native range (Western Atlantic / Caribbean / Eastern Pacific), wild adult height (up to 20 m), viviparous propagule biology, prop-root structure naming, and intertidal habitat. Not used for husbandry parameters.
